title: Saving DOE’s Workforce Act
plain_summary: The Saving DOE's Workforce Act stops the Department of Energy from laying off workers or firing them until Congress creates a complete budget for 2026. The only time the Department of Energy can still fire someone is if that person does something wrong at work or does a bad job. This law protects most Department of Energy employees from losing their jobs while Congress works on the budget. The rule will stay in place until the government has a full spending plan approved for fiscal year 2026.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving DOE's Workforce Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Reduction in Force Moratorium at Department of Energy
sections.text: The Department of Energy cannot lay off workers or fire employees until Congress passes a full budget for the department for the year twenty twenty six. During this time, the department cannot start or carry out any mass layoffs. The department also cannot force any employee to leave their job unless that employee did something wrong like misconduct, not doing their work properly, or being inefficient at their job. This rule applies to employees in the competitive service (workers who got their jobs through testing and competition), employees in the excepted service (workers hired outside the usual competitive process), and career appointees in the Senior Executive Service (high level career managers). These terms have the same meanings as defined in sections twenty one oh two, twenty one oh three, and thirty one thirty two of title five of the United States Code. This rule works along with any other existing laws about taking action against employees, including chapter seventy five of title five.
